topic: Acids, Bases, Salts & pH вЂ” Properties, Indicators & Key Compounds

questions.text: What is the chemical name of 'oil of vitriol' and what is it used for?
options: Nitric acid (HNOв‚ѓ) вЂ” used to make explosives (TNT) and fertilisers
Sulphuric acid (Hв‚‚SOв‚„) вЂ” used in car batteries, manufacture of fertilisers, dyes, drugs, and as a dehydrating agent
Hydrochloric acid (HCl) вЂ” used in cleaning and stomach acid
Phosphoric acid (Hв‚ѓPOв‚„) вЂ” used in soft drinks as a sour flavouring agent
questions.explanation: Oil of vitriol is the old alchemical name for concentrated sulphuric acid (Hв‚‚SOв‚„). It was called 'oil of vitriol' because it is a viscous, oily liquid and was originally obtained by heating green vitriol (FeSOв‚„В·7Hв‚‚O). Hв‚‚SOв‚„ is the most important industrial chemical вЂ” often called the 'king of chemicals'. Uses: Car/lead-acid batteries (dilute Hв‚‚SOв‚„ is the electrolyte); Manufacture of fertilisers (superphosphate, ammonium sulphate); Manufacture of dyes, drugs, explosives, and rayon; Dehydrating agent (absorbs water from organic substances вЂ” chars sugar, dehydrates organic tissue); Petroleum refining; Acid rain formation (SOв‚ѓ + Hв‚‚O в†’ Hв‚‚SOв‚„). Hв‚‚SOв‚„ is a strong diprotic acid (donates 2 HвЃє). Other vitriols: Green vitriol = FeSOв‚„В·7Hв‚‚O (ferrous sulphate); Blue vitriol = CuSOв‚„В·5Hв‚‚O (copper sulphate); White vitriol = ZnSOв‚„В·7Hв‚‚O.
questions.source: SSC CGL / SSC CHSL previous years вЂ” 'oil of vitriol' = sulphuric acid is very frequently asked

questions.text: Which acid is present in vinegar?
options: Citric acid
Lactic acid
Acetic acid (ethanoic acid)
Tartaric acid
questions.explanation: Vinegar contains 5вЂ“8% acetic acid (ethanoic acid, CHв‚ѓCOOH) in water. Acetic acid is a weak organic acid with a characteristic pungent sour smell. It was historically called 'acetum' in Latin (meaning 'sharp/sour' вЂ” same root as 'acid' and 'acet-' in acetyl, acetone, etc.). Vinegar is produced by fermentation: Ethanol (in wine/beer) + oxygen в†’ acetic acid + water (oxidation by Acetobacter bacteria). Uses of acetic acid: Food preservative in pickles and sauces; Coagulating agent in rubber production; Starting material for making various chemicals; As glacial acetic acid (100% pure, solidifies at 16.7В°C). Other natural acids: Citric acid (Cв‚†Hв‚€Oв‚‡) = lemon, orange, citrus fruits; Tartaric acid = tamarind (imli), grapes; Oxalic acid = tomato, spinach, tea; Lactic acid = curd (yogurt), sour milk, muscles during exercise; Formic acid = red ants, nettle sting.
questions.source: SSC CGL / SSC CHSL previous years вЂ” acetic acid (ethanoic acid) in vinegar is very frequently asked

questions.text: What happens when blue litmus paper is dipped into an acidic solution?
options: The blue litmus turns colourless
The blue litmus turns red
The blue litmus turns green
The blue litmus remains unchanged (blue)
questions.explanation: Litmus is a natural indicator obtained from lichens (Rocella tinctoria) and is the most commonly used acid-base indicator. In acidic solutions (pH < 7): BLUE litmus turns RED; In basic/alkaline solutions (pH > 7): RED litmus turns BLUE. Memory trick: B-R-B (Blue turns Red in acid, Base makes Red turn Blue) or simply: 'Acids are RED alert вЂ” they turn litmus red.' The change is caused by the HвЃє ions in acid protonating the litmus molecules, changing their structure and hence their colour. Litmus works in the pH range of approximately 5 to 8. For SSC exams: Blue litmus в†’ Red = test for acid; Red litmus в†’ Blue = test for base. Neutral solutions (pH 7) don't change either litmus paper. Litmus is also available as litmus solution (purple) which turns red in acid and blue in base.
questions.source: SSC CGL / SSC CHSL previous years вЂ” blue litmus turns red in acid is very frequently asked

questions.text: Baking soda (NaHCOв‚ѓ) is used in cooking. What gas is produced when baking soda is heated or reacts with acid?
options: Oxygen (Oв‚‚) вЂ” baking soda decomposes to release pure oxygen
Carbon dioxide (COв‚‚) вЂ” makes dough rise; also produced with acids in baking powder
Hydrogen (Hв‚‚) вЂ” the lightest gas; makes cakes light and fluffy
Nitrogen dioxide (NOв‚‚) вЂ” a reddish-brown gas that aerates the dough
questions.explanation: Baking soda (NaHCOв‚ѓ, sodium bicarbonate) releases COв‚‚ in two situations: On heating: 2NaHCOв‚ѓ в†’ Naв‚‚COв‚ѓ + Hв‚‚O + COв‚‚в†‘ (thermal decomposition); With acid (as in baking powder): NaHCOв‚ѓ + HвЃє в†’ NaвЃє + Hв‚‚O + COв‚‚в†‘. The COв‚‚ gas gets trapped in the dough/batter в†’ small bubbles в†’ dough rises (leavening). Baking powder = baking soda + tartaric acid (cream of tartar) or citric acid. The acid in baking powder neutralises the Naв‚‚COв‚ѓ left over from heating (otherwise the cake would taste bitter and soapy). Uses of NaHCOв‚ѓ: Baking/cooking (leavening agent); Antacid (neutralises excess stomach acid вЂ” mild base, pH ~8.3: NaHCOв‚ѓ + HCl в†’ NaCl + Hв‚‚O + COв‚‚в†‘); Fire extinguishers (dry powder type вЂ” heat decomposes NaHCOв‚ѓ в†’ COв‚‚ smothers fire); Mild cleaning agent. Note: Naв‚‚COв‚ѓ (washing soda) в‰  NaHCOв‚ѓ (baking soda) вЂ” two different compounds with different uses.
questions.source: SSC CGL / SSC CHSL previous years вЂ” baking soda produces COв‚‚ on heating or with acid is very frequently asked

questions.text: Aqua regia can dissolve gold and platinum вЂ” what is its composition and why can't either acid alone dissolve these noble metals?
options: Aqua regia is a mixture of concentrated Hв‚‚SOв‚„ and HNOв‚ѓ (3:1); neither alone can oxidise gold because it takes extremely strong acid

Aqua regia is a freshly prepared mixture of concentrated HNOв‚ѓ (1 part) and concentrated HCl (3 parts); it works because HNOв‚ѓ oxidises gold while HCl provides ClвЃ» ions that form soluble tetrachloroaurate complex AuClв‚„вЃ», pulling the gold into solution
Aqua regia contains HF which forms stable gold fluoride complexes; it is made by mixing HF and HNOв‚ѓ in a 1:1 ratio
Aqua regia is super-heated Hв‚‚SOв‚„ (above 200В°C); the gold dissolves because Hв‚‚SOв‚„ is a stronger acid at high temperatures
questions.explanation: Aqua regia (Latin: 'royal water', named by alchemists because it dissolves the 'royal metals') = 1 part concentrated HNOв‚ѓ + 3 parts concentrated HCl, freshly mixed. Why it works when neither acid alone can dissolve gold: HNOв‚ѓ is a strong oxidising agent вЂ” it can oxidise AuвЃ° to AuВівЃє: Au в†’ AuВівЃє + 3eвЃ». But AuВівЃє is such a noble (unreactive) ion it would simply deposit back. HCl provides ClвЃ» ions that immediately complex with AuВівЃє: AuВівЃє + 4ClвЃ» в†’ [AuClв‚„]вЃ» (tetrachloroaurate, soluble). The complex formation drives the dissolution forward. Nitrosyl chloride (NOCl) is also released and is a very powerful oxidant. Fun fact: During WWII, physicist Niels Bohr dissolved Nobel Prize gold medals (belonging to colleagues) in aqua regia to hide them from the Nazis; after the war, the gold was precipitated back and new medals made from it. Platinum dissolves in aqua regia as: Pt + 4HCl + HNOв‚ѓ в†’ Hв‚‚PtClв‚„ + NOCl + 2Hв‚‚O.
questions.source: SSC CGL вЂ” aqua regia composition (HNOв‚ѓ:HCl = 1:3) and why it dissolves gold is a tricky question

questions.text: What is the difference between 'hard water' and 'soft water', and why does soap not lather in hard water?
options: Hard water has a higher density than soft water; soap molecules are too light to float in hard water

Hard water contains dissolved CaВІвЃє and MgВІвЃє salts; soap (sodium stearate, Cв‚Ѓв‚‡Hв‚ѓв‚…COONa) reacts with these ions to form insoluble calcium/magnesium stearate ('scum'), consuming the soap before any lather can form
Hard water has a lower temperature than soft water; soap only lathers in warm water above 25В°C
Soft water has dissolved COв‚‚; the COв‚‚ reacts with soap to produce foam; hard water has no dissolved gas
questions.explanation: Hard water contains dissolved CaВІвЃє (from CaClв‚‚, CaSOв‚„, Ca(HCOв‚ѓ)в‚‚) and MgВІвЃє (from MgClв‚‚, MgSOв‚„, Mg(HCOв‚ѓ)в‚‚). When soap (sodium stearate, Cв‚Ѓв‚‡Hв‚ѓв‚…COOвЃ»NaвЃє) is added to hard water: 2Cв‚Ѓв‚‡Hв‚ѓв‚…COOвЃ»NaвЃє + CaВІвЃє в†’ (Cв‚Ѓв‚‡Hв‚ѓв‚…COO)в‚‚Caв†“ (insoluble calcium stearate вЂ” 'scum') + 2NaвЃє. The soap forms an insoluble precipitate with CaВІвЃє/MgВІвЃє and is destroyed before lather can form. This wastes soap. Types: Temporary hardness (Ca(HCOв‚ѓ)в‚‚, Mg(HCOв‚ѓ)в‚‚) вЂ” removed by boiling: Ca(HCOв‚ѓ)в‚‚ в†’ CaCOв‚ѓв†“ + Hв‚‚O + COв‚‚в†‘ (scale/fur in kettles); Permanent hardness (CaSOв‚„, MgSOв‚„, CaClв‚‚) вЂ” cannot be removed by boiling; removed by: Adding Naв‚‚COв‚ѓ (washing soda): CaClв‚‚ + Naв‚‚COв‚ѓ в†’ CaCOв‚ѓв†“ + 2NaCl; Ion exchange resins (zeolites); Distillation. Detergents (synthetic surfactants with sulphonate groups) work in hard water because calcium sulphonate IS soluble вЂ” unlike soap scum.
questions.source: SSC CGL вЂ” hard vs soft water and soap-scum reaction in hard water is a tricky application question

questions.text: Why is NaHCOв‚ѓ (baking soda) used as an antacid, while Naв‚‚COв‚ѓ (washing soda) is NOT used for the same purpose?
options: NaHCOв‚ѓ and Naв‚‚COв‚ѓ are both used as antacids with equal effectiveness; the choice is merely a matter of availability

NaHCOв‚ѓ is a MILD base (pH ~8.3) that gently neutralises excess stomach acid (HCl) without being too alkaline; Naв‚‚COв‚ѓ is a STRONG base (pH ~11.6) that would seriously irritate or corrode the stomach lining if ingested
Naв‚‚COв‚ѓ cannot neutralise HCl because it is a salt, not a base; only true bases like NaHCOв‚ѓ can neutralise acids
Washing soda is used for heavy-duty neutralisation; baking soda is used only for mild cases of acid reflux because of its pleasant taste
questions.explanation: Both NaHCOв‚ѓ and Naв‚‚COв‚ѓ can neutralise HCl (stomach acid): NaHCOв‚ѓ + HCl в†’ NaCl + Hв‚‚O + COв‚‚в†‘ (mild, gentle вЂ” pH of solution ~8.3); Naв‚‚COв‚ѓ + 2HCl в†’ 2NaCl + Hв‚‚O + COв‚‚в†‘ (also effective, but pH ~11вЂ“12 if excess). The critical difference: NaHCOв‚ѓ is a MILD base вЂ” excess NaHCOв‚ѓ makes a solution of pH ~8.3, only mildly alkaline. Even if excess is ingested, it is safe. Naв‚‚COв‚ѓ (washing soda) is a MUCH STRONGER base вЂ” it can cause: Chemical burns to esophagus and stomach lining; Hypernatraemia (sodium poisoning); Severe gastrointestinal irritation. Also, Naв‚‚COв‚ѓ is sodium CARBONATE (dibasic, neutralises 2HвЃє per molecule) вЂ” far more alkaline than the monobasic NaHCOв‚ѓ. Similarly: Mg(OH)в‚‚ (milk of magnesia) is used as antacid вЂ” mild base, insoluble (low absorption into blood). Ca(OH)в‚‚ (slaked lime) is a base but too corrosive for antacid use.
questions.source: SSC CGL вЂ” why baking soda is an antacid but washing soda is NOT (pH and corrosiveness) is a tricky question

questions.text: What is potash alum [KAl(SOв‚„)в‚‚В·12Hв‚‚O], and how does it purify water?
options: Alum is a disinfectant that kills bacteria by releasing chlorine when dissolved in water

Alum is a coagulant: when dissolved in water, AlВівЃє ions hydrolyse to form Al(OH)в‚ѓ вЂ” a sticky, gelatinous precipitate that traps suspended mud, bacteria, and impurities, causing them to flocculate (clump) and settle
Alum creates an acidic environment that neutralises alkaline impurities in drinking water, making it safe to drink
Alum is a reducing agent that removes dissolved oxygen from water, killing aerobic bacteria
questions.explanation: Potash alum = KAl(SOв‚„)в‚‚В·12Hв‚‚O (potassium aluminium sulphate dodecahydrate). How it purifies water вЂ” the coagulation process: AlВівЃє + 3Hв‚‚O в†’ Al(OH)в‚ѓ + 3HвЃє. Al(OH)в‚ѓ is a gelatinous (jelly-like), sticky floc. This floc: Physically traps fine suspended particles (clay, silt, bacteria) through adsorption; The + charged Al(OH)в‚ѓ attracts and neutralises в€’ charged colloidal particles (most suspended impurities carry negative surface charges); Particles clump together (flocculate) and settle to the bottom (sedimentation) where they can be filtered out. This is the alum-coagulation-flocculation-sedimentation-filtration sequence used in municipal water treatment. Traditional uses: Village wells вЂ” handful of alum powder thrown into the well causes mud to settle overnight; As a styptic pencil (haemostatic) вЂ” alum blocks cut вЂ” AlВівЃє causes proteins in blood to coagulate, stopping bleeding; After-shave block вЂ” same coagulation mechanism seals microscopic cuts from shaving; Pickling vegetables (keeps them crisp вЂ” AlВівЃє firms cell walls).
questions.source: SSC CGL вЂ” alum (potash alum) purifying water by coagulation/flocculation is a tricky application question

questions.text: Why does the pH of pure water change with temperature, even though it remains 'neutral'?
options: Pure water's pH doesn't change with temperature вЂ” pH 7 is the absolute definition of neutral at all temperatures

Water undergoes autoionisation: Hв‚‚O в‡Њ HвЃє + OHвЃ»; at higher temperatures, this equilibrium shifts right, producing more HвЃє and OHвЃ» ions. At 25В°C, pH = 7.0 (neutral). At 100В°C, pH в‰€ 6.1 (but still neutral, because [HвЃє] = [OHвЃ»]). Neutral doesn't always mean pH 7 вЂ” it means [HвЃє] = [OHвЃ»].
Pure water at higher temperatures absorbs COв‚‚ from the air more readily, which forms carbonic acid and lowers the pH
The pH meter's electrode changes its reading at higher temperatures due to thermal drift, giving an artificially lower reading for pure water
questions.explanation: This is a deeply underrated and misunderstood concept: Pure water self-ionises: Hв‚‚O в‡Њ HвЃє + OHвЃ» (Kw = [HвЃє][OHвЃ»]). At 25В°C: Kw = 1Г—10вЃ»В№вЃґ; [HвЃє] = [OHвЃ»] = 10вЃ»вЃ· M; pH = 7.0. At 100В°C: Kw = 5.1Г—10вЃ»В№Ві; [HвЃє] = [OHвЃ»] = 2.25Г—10вЃ»вЃ· M; pH = 6.65. The water is still NEUTRAL (equal HвЃє and OHвЃ») but the pH has decreased. Definition of neutral: [HвЃє] = [OHвЃ»] вЂ” not necessarily pH 7. pH 7 is neutral ONLY at 25В°C. This is why: blood pH of 7.4 at body temperature (37В°C) is slightly alkaline; the neutral pH at 37В°C is ~6.8, so blood pH 7.4 is indeed basic/alkaline relative to neutral at that temperature. For SSC exams: pH 7 = neutral is correct at standard conditions (25В°C). This subtlety appears in higher-level examinations.
questions.source: SSC CGL вЂ” pH of neutral water changes with temperature (autoionisation) is underrated but conceptually important

questions.text: What is a 'buffer solution', and why is it important for blood?
options: A buffer is a highly concentrated acid that prevents any base from changing the pH; blood uses concentrated HCl as its buffer
A buffer is a solution that resists changes in pH when small amounts of acid or base are added; blood uses a carbonic acid-bicarbonate buffer (Hв‚‚COв‚ѓ/HCOв‚ѓвЃ») to maintain pH 7.35вЂ“7.45
A buffer absorbs all acids produced in the body and converts them to neutral salts permanently; the buffer is consumed and needs to be replaced by food
A buffer is a protein that carries acid-base molecules in the blood; haemoglobin acts as the only buffer in blood
questions.explanation: A buffer solution resists changes in pH when small amounts of acid (HвЃє) or base (OHвЃ») are added. It consists of either: Weak acid + its conjugate base (e.g., CHв‚ѓCOOH + CHв‚ѓCOOвЃ»NaвЃє); OR Weak base + its conjugate acid (e.g., NHв‚ѓ + NHв‚„Cl). Blood buffer system вЂ” the carbonic acid-bicarbonate buffer: Hв‚‚COв‚ѓ в‡Њ HвЃє + HCOв‚ѓвЃ». When acid (HвЃє) enters blood: HCOв‚ѓвЃ» + HвЃє в†’ Hв‚‚COв‚ѓ в†’ COв‚‚ + Hв‚‚O (exhaled by lungs) в†’ pH restored. When base (OHвЃ») enters blood: Hв‚‚COв‚ѓ + OHвЃ» в†’ HCOв‚ѓвЃ» + Hв‚‚O в†’ pH restored. Normal blood pH: 7.35вЂ“7.45. Acidosis (pH < 7.35): can cause coma, death вЂ” occurs in uncontrolled diabetes, respiratory failure. Alkalosis (pH > 7.45): muscle cramps, seizures вЂ” occurs in hyperventilation. Blood has three buffer systems: bicarbonate (most important), haemoglobin buffer, phosphate buffer. This is why blood maintains its pH in a tight range despite metabolic activity constantly producing COв‚‚ and lactic acid.
questions.source: SSC CGL вЂ” buffer solutions and the blood carbonic acid-bicarbonate buffer system is underrated

questions.text: What is the difference between a salt hydrate (like CuSOв‚„В·5Hв‚‚O) and an anhydrous salt?
options: A salt hydrate is dissolved in water; an anhydrous salt is dissolved in an organic solvent

A salt hydrate contains chemically bound water molecules (water of crystallisation) within its crystal structure; anhydrous salts have no such water. CuSOв‚„В·5Hв‚‚O (blue) loses its water of crystallisation on heating в†’ anhydrous CuSOв‚„ (white). The rehydration test (adds water в†’ turns blue) is used to detect moisture.
Salt hydrates are formed only with sulphate salts; chloride salts are always anhydrous
Anhydrous salts are always more reactive than their hydrated forms because water molecules block the reactive sites
questions.explanation: Water of crystallisation: fixed number of water molecules chemically incorporated into the crystal lattice of a salt. The water molecules form coordinate bonds with the metal ions or hydrogen bonds with the anions. Examples: CuSOв‚„В·5Hв‚‚O (blue vitriol вЂ” blue crystals) в†’ heat в†’ CuSOв‚„ (anhydrous copper sulphate вЂ” white powder) + 5Hв‚‚O. This reaction is the basis of a key test: white anhydrous CuSOв‚„ turns blue when water is added в†’ used to detect water/moisture in liquids. Similarly: CaSOв‚„В·2Hв‚‚O (gypsum, 2 water molecules) в†’ heat at 120В°C в†’ (CaSOв‚„)в‚‚В·Hв‚‚O (plaster of paris, ВЅ water per CaSOв‚„) в†’ heat above 200В°C в†’ CaSOв‚„ (anhydrous). Naв‚‚COв‚ѓВ·10Hв‚‚O (washing soda, 10 water molecules) в†’ heat в†’ Naв‚‚COв‚ѓ (soda ash, anhydrous). MgSOв‚„В·7Hв‚‚O (Epsom salt, 7 water molecules) вЂ” used in bath salts and laxatives. The formula В·nHв‚‚O indicates n molecules of water per formula unit in the crystal.
questions.source: SSC CGL вЂ” water of crystallisation in hydrated salts (CuSOв‚„В·5Hв‚‚O vs anhydrous CuSOв‚„) is underrated

questions.text: Which acid is responsible for the pain of a bee sting versus a red ant sting?
options: Both bee stings and ant stings involve sulphuric acid вЂ” the concentration differs (bee sting is more concentrated)

Red ant sting: formic acid (methanoic acid, HCOOH) injected by the ant. Bee sting: complex mixture including melittin (a protein), histamine, and formic acid вЂ” formic acid is the burning/acidic component in BOTH cases.
Bee stings involve lactic acid; ant stings involve tartaric acid вЂ” both found naturally in insects
Both bee and ant stings use nitric acid вЂ” insects produce HNOв‚ѓ in a specialised venom gland
questions.explanation: Formic acid (methanoic acid, HCOOH) is the simplest carboxylic acid. It is responsible for the acidic burning sensation in: Red ant (Formica species) stings: ants inject formic acid through their sting. The name 'formic' comes from Latin 'formica' (ant). Nettle plant (Urtica dioica) stings: fine hollow hairs (trichomes) on nettle leaves inject a mixture containing formic acid, histamine, and serotonin when touched. Some bee stings: formic acid is one of the acidic components (the full venom cocktail includes melittin, apamin, phospholipase A2, histamine). Wasp stings are ALKALINE (contain alkalis), not acidic вЂ” this is why: bee sting (acidic) в†’ apply baking soda (base) to neutralise; wasp sting (alkaline) в†’ apply vinegar (acid) to neutralise. Formic acid is used industrially: in leather tanning, textile dyeing, antibacterial food preservative (in Europe), rubber coagulation.
questions.source: SSC CGL вЂ” formic acid in ant stings and nettles; bee sting acidic vs wasp sting alkaline is underrated

questions.text: What is the chemical formula of 'washing soda' and 'baking soda'?
options: Washing soda = NaHCOв‚ѓ; Baking soda = Naв‚‚COв‚ѓ
Washing soda = Naв‚‚COв‚ѓВ·10Hв‚‚O (sodium carbonate decahydrate); Baking soda = NaHCOв‚ѓ (sodium bicarbonate)
Washing soda = Naв‚‚SOв‚„В·10Hв‚‚O; Baking soda = NaHCOв‚ѓ
Washing soda = NaCl; Baking soda = Naв‚‚COв‚ѓ
questions.explanation: Two most commonly confused sodium compounds: Washing soda = Sodium carbonate decahydrate = Naв‚‚COв‚ѓВ·10Hв‚‚O. Also called 'soda ash' when anhydrous (Naв‚‚COв‚ѓ). Uses: Water softening (removes CaВІвЃє, MgВІвЃє by precipitation as CaCOв‚ѓ, MgCOв‚ѓ); Laundry cleaning; Glass making; Paper manufacturing; pH ~11 (strongly basic). Baking soda = Sodium bicarbonate = NaHCOв‚ѓ. Also called 'sodium hydrogen carbonate'. Uses: Leavening agent in baking (releases COв‚‚ в†’ dough rises); Antacid (neutralises excess stomach acid); Fire extinguisher (dry powder type); Mild cleaning agent; pH ~8.3 (mildly basic). Key differences: Naв‚‚COв‚ѓ is dibasic (2 OHвЃ» equivalents per formula unit); NaHCOв‚ѓ is monobasic (1 OHвЃ» equivalent); Naв‚‚COв‚ѓ is MUCH more alkaline than NaHCOв‚ѓ; Naв‚‚COв‚ѓ has 10 water molecules (decahydrate); NaHCOв‚ѓ is anhydrous. Never mix them up in an exam вЂ” the most frequently tested distinction in salt chemistry.
questions.source: SSC CGL / SSC CHSL вЂ” washing soda (Naв‚‚COв‚ѓВ·10Hв‚‚O) vs baking soda (NaHCOв‚ѓ) is very frequently asked

questions.text: What is the pH of a neutral solution at 25В°C, and what does a pH below 7 indicate?
options: Neutral pH = 0; pH below 0 = acidic; pH above 0 = basic
Neutral pH = 7; pH below 7 = acidic solution (excess HвЃє); pH above 7 = alkaline/basic solution (excess OHвЃ»)
Neutral pH = 14; pH below 14 = acidic; any solution with pH = 14 is neutral
Neutral pH = 7; pH below 7 = basic; pH above 7 = acidic
questions.explanation: The pH scale (0вЂ“14 at 25В°C): pH 7 = neutral (pure water at 25В°C; equal HвЃє and OHвЃ» concentration = 10вЃ»вЃ· mol/L each). pH < 7 = acidic (more HвЃє than OHвЃ»; lower the pH, more acidic): pH 1вЂ“3 = strong acids; pH 4вЂ“6 = weak acids. pH > 7 = alkaline/basic (more OHвЃ» than HвЃє; higher the pH, more basic): pH 8вЂ“10 = weak bases; pH 11вЂ“14 = strong bases. Each unit decrease in pH = 10-fold INCREASE in [HвЃє] (logarithmic scale): pH 5 is 10Г— more acidic than pH 6; pH 3 is 100Г— more acidic than pH 5. Common pH values for SSC: Blood = 7.35вЂ“7.45; Gastric juice = 1.5вЂ“2.0; Lemon juice = 2.0; Vinegar = 3.0; Milk = 6.5; Pure water = 7.0; Seawater = 8.2; Baking soda = 8.3; Toothpaste = ~9; Household ammonia = 11; Bleach = 12; Caustic soda = 14. Normal rain = ~5.6 (slightly acidic due to dissolved COв‚‚); Acid rain < 5.6 (due to SOв‚‚ and NOв‚“ from industrial pollution).
questions.source: SSC CGL / SSC CHSL вЂ” pH scale, neutral at 7, pH below 7 = acidic is very frequently asked

questions.text: Blue vitriol is the common name for which compound, and what colour does it turn when heated?
options: Blue vitriol = FeSOв‚„В·7Hв‚‚O (ferrous sulphate); it turns red when heated
Blue vitriol = CuSOв‚„В·5Hв‚‚O (copper sulphate pentahydrate); it turns white (anhydrous CuSOв‚„) on strong heating when it loses its water of crystallisation
Blue vitriol = CuClв‚‚В·2Hв‚‚O (copper chloride dihydrate); it turns brown when heated
Blue vitriol = Co(NOв‚ѓ)в‚‚В·6Hв‚‚O (cobalt nitrate); it turns pink when heated due to loss of water
questions.explanation: Blue vitriol = copper(II) sulphate pentahydrate = CuSOв‚„В·5Hв‚‚O. It is: Bright blue crystalline solid (due to the 5 water molecules coordinated to CuВІвЃє in the crystal вЂ” the blue colour comes from d-d electronic transitions in the CuВІвЃє ion complexed with water). When heated strongly: CuSOв‚„В·5Hв‚‚O в†’ CuSOв‚„ (anhydrous) + 5Hв‚‚O; The anhydrous CuSOв‚„ is WHITE/off-white powder (no water в†’ no CuВІвЃє hydration в†’ no colour from d-d transitions). This is the basis of a key test: White anhydrous CuSOв‚„ turns BLUE when water is added в†’ used to test for presence of water. When water is added back: CuSOв‚„ (white) + 5Hв‚‚O в†’ CuSOв‚„В·5Hв‚‚O (blue). Uses of CuSOв‚„: Bordeaux mixture (CuSOв‚„ + Ca(OH)в‚‚) = fungicide in vineyards and farms; Electroplating of copper; Laboratory reagent (Fehling's solution for detecting reducing sugars); Germicide in swimming pools. Other vitriols: Green vitriol = FeSOв‚„В·7Hв‚‚O (pale green); White vitriol = ZnSOв‚„В·7Hв‚‚O.
questions.source: SSC CGL / SSC CHSL вЂ” blue vitriol = CuSOв‚„В·5Hв‚‚O, white when heated (water of crystallisation lost) is very frequently asked

questions.text: Which acid is produced in the stomach for digestion, and what common problem results from its excess?
options: Sulphuric acid (Hв‚‚SOв‚„) вЂ” excess causes peptic ulcers; treated with antacids
Hydrochloric acid (HCl) вЂ” excess production causes hyperacidity/acid reflux (GERD); treated with antacids like NaHCOв‚ѓ, Mg(OH)в‚‚, or Al(OH)в‚ѓ
Lactic acid (Cв‚ѓHв‚†Oв‚ѓ) вЂ” excess causes stomach cramps; only produced during vigorous exercise
Nitric acid (HNOв‚ѓ) вЂ” excess causes stomach burning; treated by drinking milk which contains calcium that neutralises it
questions.explanation: The stomach produces hydrochloric acid (HCl) from parietal cells in the gastric mucosa. Normal gastric pH = 1.5вЂ“2.0 (very acidic). Functions of stomach HCl: Activates pepsinogen to pepsin (protein-digesting enzyme); Kills harmful microorganisms in food; Creates acidic environment for pepsin to work optimally; Helps absorb B12, iron, calcium. Excess HCl causes: Hyperacidity / acid reflux / GERD (gastroesophageal reflux disease): burning sensation in chest (heartburn); Peptic ulcers: erosion of stomach lining; Gastritis: inflammation of stomach lining. Antacids (neutralise excess HCl): NaHCOв‚ѓ + HCl в†’ NaCl + Hв‚‚O + COв‚‚в†‘ (baking soda); Mg(OH)в‚‚ + 2HCl в†’ MgClв‚‚ + 2Hв‚‚O (milk of magnesia вЂ” safest, no gas); Al(OH)в‚ѓ + 3HCl в†’ AlClв‚ѓ + 3Hв‚‚O (aluminium hydroxide). Commercial antacids: ENO = NaHCOв‚ѓ + citric acid (produces COв‚‚ fizz); Gelusil, Digene = Al(OH)в‚ѓ + Mg(OH)в‚‚ mixture; Omeprazole is a proton pump inhibitor вЂ” reduces HCl production itself (not an antacid in the traditional sense).
questions.source: SSC CGL / SSC CHSL вЂ” HCl in stomach, excess в†’ hyperacidity, treated by antacids is very frequently asked
